narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model focused on predicting your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model assessed your overall profile and estimated a 59% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection since the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed and avoid high failure risks. The decision hinged on just a few key factors from your background, with your Year 3 school decile ranking as the top influence, strongly pushing the prediction toward failure because your position in the bottom decile (1 out of 10) signals weaker performance compared to peers. Next, your undergraduate GPA played a major counterbalancing role, pulling strongly in the direction of passing due to its above-average strength, which the model views as a positive indicator of academic potential. Your LSAT score of 26, well below the average of about 37, came in third as an influential factor, nudging the prediction toward failure since lower scores like yours correlate with bar struggles in the model's view. While your middle family income bracket and full-time student status align with most applicants, they didn't sway the outcome much here. Compared to typical students, your early Year 1 decile of 3 was below average too, but it wasn't as pivotal as your later performance dip. This combination painted a picture of inconsistent academic momentum that outweighed your GPA strength for this prediction. It's tough to hear, especially with your solid undergrad record, but these patterns are what tipped the scales. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or explanations, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your profile favorably, as even small improvements in top factors often change model outcomes. Many applicants in similar spots succeed by targeting these areas— you've got a strong foundation to build on.
